summary: Ukraine endured a massive coordinated drone and missile barrage across multiple oblasts, with the most intense strikes hitting Kyiv, Kharkiv, and the western rail network. Russian forces employed new “Geranium” swarm tactics along the Belarus border to overwhelm air defenses, while Ukrainian units reported successful interceptions and limited counter‑strikes on logistics nodes. Civilian casualties rose sharply, with at least 32 dead and dozens injured in Kyiv alone, and critical infrastructure in several regions knocked out. Internationally, the EU extended temporary protection for Ukrainian refugees to 2028 and the U.S. confirmed a deployment of military advisors to study Ukrainian drone warfare.
key_points: Over 1,000 Russian UAVs launched in a single day, targeting Kyiv, western oblasts and railway hubs, with Ukrainian air defenses downing 41 missiles and 652 drones.
Russian “Geranium” swarm drones flew 5‑10 km from the Belarus border in dense formations to saturate Ukrainian SAM systems.
At least 32 civilians killed and over 150 injured in Kyiv and surrounding regions during the night‑time attack, including one child.
EU extended temporary protection for Ukrainian refugees until March 2028, signaling continued political support.
U.S. Pentagon confirmed deployment of American military advisors to Ukraine to study drone tactics and air‑defence integration.
casualties_mentioned: At least 32 civilians killed (including one child) and dozens injured in Kyiv; 2 civilians killed in Zdolbuniv railway strike; 2 civilians killed in Kherson bus attack; multiple injuries reported across Kharkiv, Odesa, Rivne, Cherkasy and other oblasts.
territorial: No significant territorial changes reported; Russian attacks focused on infrastructure and urban centers rather than ground advances.

sections.executive_summary: The past 24 hours saw an unprecedented surge in Russian aerial attacks, with over a thousand UAVs and dozens of missiles targeting critical infrastructure across Ukraine. Ukrainian air defenses performed robustly, intercepting the majority of threats, but civilian casualties rose sharply, especially in Kyiv. Russia introduced a new swarm‑drone tactic from the Belarus border, aiming to saturate defenses and enable deeper strikes. The EU extended refugee protection, and the United States dispatched advisors to study Ukrainian drone warfare, indicating sustained Western engagement.

ukraine.title: Ukraine
ukraine.subtitle: Ukrainian defensive operations and strikes
points: Ukrainian Air Defence Command reported shooting down 41 of 56 incoming missiles and 652 of 675 UAVs during the night‑time barrage, concentrating assets around Kyiv, Kharkiv and Odesa.

The Ukrainian 8th Air Assault Brigade claimed a successful strike on a Russian logistics depot near Dnipro, destroying a convoy of three fuel trucks, as corroborated by local Odesa administration reports.
Ukrainian special forces reportedly sabotaged a Russian railway bridge in Zdolbuniv, disrupting a supply line and causing two civilian fatalities, confirmed by the regional governor.
The Ministry of Defence announced the deployment of Palantir analytics platforms to improve real‑time targeting, following a briefing with CEO Alex Karp and President Zelensky.
Ukrainian forces repelled a Russian ground assault near the village of Bachevsk in Sumy, holding the line after advancing 700 m on 19 sectors, according to the “Two Majors” daily report.

russia.title: Russia
russia.subtitle: Russian military operations and strategic posture
points: The Russian Ministry of Defence disclosed the use of “Geranium” swarm drones, launching them in groups of 50‑100 from 5‑10 km off the Belarus border to overwhelm Ukrainian SAMs.
Moscow claimed the destruction of 23 Ukrainian UAVs over the Kharkiv region and the neutralisation of two Ukrainian artillery batteries near Luhansk.
Russian forces reported a combined air‑strike on the energy facility in Zhovkva, Lvov region, leaving the city without power; the attack was confirmed by the local mayor.
The Russian Air Force announced the loss of 92 drones across multiple fronts on 13‑14 May, indicating high attrition rates for their UAV fleet.
A Russian FPV drone strike on a passenger train in Belgorod Oblast derailed the rail line, injuring three railway workers, highlighting the cross‑border impact of the campaign.

eastern_front.title: Eastern Front
eastern_front.subtitle: Donetsk & Luhansk combat operations
points: Ukrainian troops of the 57th Motorized Brigade reported repelling a Russian infantry assault near Marinka, inflicting 12 enemy KIA and destroying two BMP‑2s.
Russian artillery shelled the settlement of Vodyane in Donetsk, causing minor damage but no civilian casualties, according to the Donetsk People’s Republic (unconfirmed).
Ukrainian reconnaissance drones identified a new Russian forward command post near Avdiivka, prompting a precision strike that destroyed a command vehicle.
The Luhansk People's Militia claimed to have captured a small hamlet east of Luhansk, but Ukrainian sources deny any territorial loss.
Heavy fighting continued around the village of Bakhmut, with both sides reporting continued artillery duels and no decisive breakthrough.

northern_front.title: Northern Front
northern_front.subtitle: Kharkiv region and Sumy border activity
points: Ukrainian forces in the Volchansk sector reported advancing 950 m on twelve sectors, engaging Russian infantry in close‑quarter combat.
Russian UAVs targeted the railway station of Unecha in Bryansk Oblast, wounding two Russian Railways employees; the strike was claimed as a cross‑border retaliation.
In Sumy, the Russian “Army Group North” cleared forested areas near Shostka, advancing towards Bachevsk, but Ukrainian defenders halted further progress.
Ukrainian air defenses intercepted a salvo of 15 missiles over Kharkiv, preventing damage to the regional power grid.
A Russian drone strike on a residential building in Kharkiv caused three civilian injuries, confirmed by local emergency services.

southern_front.title: Southern Front
southern_front.subtitle: Zaporizhzhia & Kherson operations
points: Russian forces launched a coordinated missile and drone wave on Zaporizhzhia, hitting the Tokmak CDH building and damaging four apartment blocks; no fatalities reported.
Ukrainian artillery returned fire on Russian positions near the village of Kirovohradka in Kherson, destroying two artillery pieces.
A Russian drone struck a critical energy facility in Zhovkva, Lvov region, cutting power to 31,000 customers; the attack was verified by the mayor.
In Odesa, a UAV‑dropped explosive hit a nine‑storey residential building, injuring two civilians and igniting parked cars.
Ukrainian special forces reported sabotaging a Russian ammunition depot near Melitopol, causing a large explosion and temporary supply disruption.

air_war.title: Air War
air_war.subtitle: Drone, missile and aviation operations
points: Russian forces launched over 1,000 UAVs in a 24‑hour period, employing “Geranium” swarm tactics to saturate Ukrainian air‑defence corridors.
Ukrainian air‑defence units downed 41 of 56 incoming missiles and intercepted 652 of 675 UAVs, achieving a 96% interception rate overall.
Russian air‑force reported the loss of 92 drones on 13‑14 May, indicating high attrition in their UAV fleet.
A Russian FPV drone strike on a passenger train in Belgorod Oblast caused three injuries, marking the first confirmed cross‑border rail attack.
Ukrainian forces used Palantir analytics to prioritize high‑value targets, resulting in a successful strike on a Russian logistics hub near Dnipro.
Electronic warfare activity increased, with Russian jamming of Ukrainian radar bands reported over the Kharkiv and Kyiv sectors.

key_developments: Over 1,000 Russian UAVs launched in a single day, overwhelming Ukrainian air defenses.
Introduction of Russian “Geranium” swarm drone tactics from the Belarus border.
Civilian death toll in Kyiv rises to at least 32, with dozens more injured across multiple oblasts.
EU extends temporary protection for Ukrainian refugees until March 2028.
U.S. Pentagon confirms deployment of American military advisors to study Ukrainian drone warfare.
Palantir analytics integrated into Ukrainian command‑and‑control for real‑time targeting.
Significant infrastructure damage in Zhovkva, Odesa and Zaporizhzhia from drone strikes.
sections.threat_assessment: Russian operational momentum remains high, driven by massed UAV swarms and saturation missile attacks aimed at critical infrastructure. Ukrainian air‑defence performance is strong but attrition of UAVs is accelerating, suggesting a possible shift to higher‑value cruise missiles in the coming days. The probability of a major ground breakthrough remains LOW given entrenched front‑line positions, but the risk of a large‑scale civilian casualty event is MODERATE due to continued urban bombardments. Key indicators to watch include any escalation in Russian ballistic missile use, changes in UAV launch rates, and the impact of Western advisory missions on Ukrainian drone tactics.
sections.regional_response: The EU formalised an extension of temporary protection for Ukrainian refugees to 2028, reinforcing political backing. The United States announced a limited deployment of military advisors focused on drone and air‑defence expertise, signalling deeper tactical cooperation. NATO allies reiterated commitment to supply additional air‑defence systems, with Germany pledging an extra 12 IRIS‑T radars. Hungary summoned the Russian ambassador for the first time since the war began, condemning the recent Transcarpathian strike, while Slovakia temporarily closed its border crossings with Ukraine for security reasons.
sections.intelligence_notes: Satellite imagery shows increased Russian UAV launch activity from bases in Kursk and Belgorod, with new launch pads constructed near the Belarus border. Logistics trails indicate a surge in fuel deliveries to forward Russian artillery units in the Kharkiv sector, suggesting preparation for sustained bombardment. Electronic‑warfare monitoring detected intensified Russian jamming of GPS and L‑band frequencies over Kyiv, likely to degrade Ukrainian navigation and targeting. Ukrainian use of Palantir platforms appears to have accelerated target prioritisation, improving strike efficiency against high‑value Russian logistics nodes. No clear evidence of large‑scale Russian reserve redeployments was observed, but the concentration of UAVs hints at a shift toward attrition‑based aerial strategy.
